title: Migrating right lower quadrant pain

chart.oneLiner: 22M presents with ~18 hours of abdominal pain that began around the umbilicus and migrated to the right lower quadrant, with nausea and anorexia.

opening: It started around my belly button yesterday and now it's settled down on my lower right side.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 22-year-old man, lying still (movement worsens pain). Cooperative.

HPI: Pain began ~18h ago, dull and periumbilical, then over hours MIGRATED to the right lower quadrant where it is now sharp and constant. Anorexia (didn't want dinner — classic), nausea, one episode of vomiting. Low-grade fever. Worse with movement, coughing, the car ride in. NO diarrhea, NO dysuria, NO prior similar episodes.
PMH: none. MEDS: none. SOCIAL: college student, social alcohol, no drugs.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: uncomfortable, lying still.
- Vitals: BP 126/74, HR 96, RR 16, 37.9°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Abdomen: maximal tenderness at McBurney's point (RLQ) with voluntary guarding and rebound tenderness; positive Rovsing sign; positive psoas sign. No distension.
- Bowel sounds present. Cardiac/lungs: normal.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the migration pattern unless asked how/where the pain started and moved.
- The peritoneal signs are revealed only if the abdomen is examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: WBC 14.2 with neutrophil predominance, Hgb 15.0, Plt 260.
labs.UA: Trace leukocytes, no nitrites, no significant hematuria.
labs.CT_abdomen: CT abdomen/pelvis: dilated, fluid-filled appendix (~10 mm) with periappendiceal fat stranding; no abscess or free air.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline; ventricular rate ~110.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline; ventricular rate ~110. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 72-year-old man reports palpitations and lightheadedness, with an irregular, rapid pulse on examination.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Small dense calcified granuloma with possible calcified hilar node (old healed granulomatous disease).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Small dense calcified granuloma with possible calcified hilar node (old healed granulomatous disease). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 58-year-old woman has a routine pre-operative film showing an incidental calcified lung nodule.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Acute appendicitis (leading,8). Mesenteric adenitis (core,2). Gastroenteritis (core,2). Ureteric colic / UTI (core,3). Inflammatory bowel disease (bonus,2). Testicular torsion / epididymitis (CANT-MISS to consider in a young male,3). Named leading dx w/ justification — migration, anorexia, peritoneal signs (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute appendicitis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Testicular torsion / epididymitis. Also reasonable: Mesenteric adenitis; Gastroenteritis; Ureteric colic / UTI.

coreActions.item: Named leading dx w/ justification — migration, anorexia, peritoneal signs

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: CBC (CANT-MISS,4). CT abdomen/pelvis (or ultrasound) (CANT-MISS,5). BMP (2). Urinalysis to exclude urinary cause (2). Lipase (1). Genital/testicular exam in a young male (bonus,1). PENALTY: shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; CBC; BMP; Urinalysis; Lipase; CT abdomen/pelvis.

bonusActions.item: Genital/testicular exam in a young male

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline, ventricular rate ~110). CREDIT: rate & rhythm — identifies the irregularly irregular rhythm (7); recognizes atrial fibrillation with RVR and absent P waves (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: calling it normal sinus / missing the abnormality (-3).
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response.
commonMistakes: calling it sinus when the rhythm is irregularly irregular
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (rate ~110); irregularly irregular, no discernible P waves.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response.

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = a small dense calcified granuloma (with possible calcified hilar node) — old healed granulomatous disease. CREDIT: identifies the calcified granuloma (5); recognizes it as a benign healed finding rather than working it up as malignancy (5). PENALTY: missing the nodule / calling the film entirely normal, or working up a clearly calcified benign nodule as cancer (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes it as a benign healed finding
commonMistakes: working up a clearly calcified benign nodule as malignancy
steps.idealAnswer: Calcified granuloma — a benign sequela of old granulomatous infection; dense uniform calcification.

coreActions: recognizes it as a benign healed finding

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the CT findings, what is your diagnosis and reasoning?
steps.rubric: Acute appendicitis (6). Cites dilated appendix with fat stranding + leukocytosis + classic exam (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute appendicitis — CT shows a dilated, fluid-filled appendix (~10 mm) with periappendiceal fat stranding, supported by the leukocytosis (WBC 14.2) and the classic migratory pain with anorexia and peritoneal signs.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH abdominal pain / surgical abdomen: NPO + IV fluids (CANT-MISS,5); broad-spectrum antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole) (5); urgent surgery consult for appendectomy (CANT-MISS,6); analgesia + antiemetics (4); serial abdominal exams (3). PENALTY: discharging a surgical abdomen (-4); delaying surgical consult / source control (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: NPO, IV fluids, analgesia, antiemetics. Broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g. ceftriaxone + metronidazole). Urgent surgical consultation for appendectomy. Serial abdominal exams.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 22M presents with ~18 hours of abdominal pain that began around the umbilicus and migrated to the right lower quadrant, with nausea and anorexia. The diagnosis is Acute appendicitis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Acute appendicitis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Acute appendicitis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalInterpretationItems: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (irregularly irregular, absent P waves, rate ~110).
Calcified granuloma — a benign, old healed granulomatous finding.
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response.
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
